prepBlurb: Angeles National Forest sits in chaparral, oak woodland, and high-elevation pine. Pack abundant water, sun protection, and long sleeves; ticks, poodle-dog bush, and poison oak are common along low-elevation trails. Forage only species you have positively identified, and avoid white sap, bitter taste, or unfamiliar berries.

entries.rationale: All parts contain urushiol oil, which causes a severe contact dermatitis. Smoke from burning plants is also toxic if inhaled.
entries.prepNotes: Avoid contact entirely. Wash exposed skin with cold soapy water within 30 minutes; launder clothing separately. Do not burn.

entries.rationale: Glandular hairs cause a delayed but severe contact dermatitis very similar to poison oak. Common in post-fire chaparral.
entries.prepNotes: Do not touch leaves, stems, or flowers. Stay on trail in burn-recovery areas; wash skin and gear after suspected contact.

entries.rationale: All parts contain tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine). Ingestion causes hallucinations, tachycardia, and can be fatal.
entries.prepNotes: Do not consume any part. Wash hands after handling.

entries.rationale: Sap contains furanocoumarins that cause phytophotodermatitis — severe blistering when sun-exposed skin contacts the plant.
entries.prepNotes: Avoid skin contact. If exposed, wash immediately and keep skin out of sunlight for 48 hours.

entries.rationale: Ripe purple berries are tart but edible. Spines on the fruit are removable and the flesh inside is safe for most people.
entries.prepNotes: Pick fully ripe (deep purple). Remove spines by rubbing with cloth or singeing briefly. Cook into jelly or eat fresh in moderation.

entries.rationale: Ripe red berries are edible after cooking. Raw berries contain small amounts of cyanogenic glycosides.
entries.prepNotes: Always cook before eating — boiling, drying, or roasting destroys the toxins. Do not consume raw.

entries.rationale: Ripe blue berries with a glaucous bloom are edible when cooked; flowers are also edible. Raw berries, leaves, and stems contain cyanogenic compounds.
entries.prepNotes: Cook berries thoroughly into syrup, jam, or pie. Discard stems, leaves, and any green or red berries. Do not eat raw.

entries.rationale: Acorns are edible after extensive leaching to remove tannins. Traditional staple food of indigenous Californians.
entries.prepNotes: Shell, grind, and leach acorn meal in repeated changes of cool water until no longer bitter. Use as flour or porridge.

entries.rationale: Flowers and young flower stalks are edible when cooked. The plant is iconic in low-elevation Angeles chaparral.
entries.prepNotes: Harvest flower buds before bloom; boil to remove bitterness. Avoid the sharp leaf tips when foraging.

entries.rationale: Dominant chaparral shrub with no edible parts. Resinous foliage is highly flammable but not toxic.
entries.prepNotes: Use only for navigation or shelter context — not a food source.

entries.rationale: Strongly aromatic but extremely bitter; not a culinary herb. Traditional medicinal use only.
entries.prepNotes: Crush a leaf to confirm the strong sage scent for ID. Do not consume in quantity.

entries.rationale: Aromatic native sage; leaves are not used as a culinary herb and over-harvesting is a conservation concern.
entries.prepNotes: Identification only. Do not harvest from public lands.
